metadata.description: kill/nice: `kill -9` force. `nice`/`renice` adjust priority. Python code examples: For `kill -9`, this is typically used in shell scripting or command-line operations, and there isn't a direct equivalent in Python for sending signals to processes. However, you can simulate the concept by terminating a process using its PID (Process ID): ```python import os import signal def kill_process(pid): try: # Send SIGKILL signal to terminate the process forcefully os.kill(pid, signal.SIGKILL) print(f"Process {pid} has been terminated.") except ProcessLookupError: print(f"No such process with PID: {pid}") # Example usage kill_process(1234) # Replace 1234 with the actual process ID you want to terminate. ``` For `nice`/`renice`, this is used to adjust the priority of a running process. In Python, you can use the `os.nice()` function to change the scheduling priority of the current process: ```python import os def set_priority(priority): try: # Set the nice value (priority) for the current process os.nice(priority) print(f"Priority has been set to: {priority}") except Exception as e: print(f"Failed to set priority: {e}") # Example usage set_priority(10) # Lower values mean higher priority. ``` Note that `os.nice()` only affects the current process and cannot be used to change the priority of other processes. For changing the priority of another process, you would typically use a system call or shell command like `renice`.
